summary: This interview focuses a lot on my experiences with ASERL, though I haven't detailed those here. ASERL has been an important part of my professional journey in librarianship, especially as I moved into leadership roles and embraced emerging technologies. My work in academic libraries and digital services likely intersected with ASERL's collaborative initiatives over the years.

My last job as a professional musician was as a staff pianist for the Milwaukee Ballet. This position was physically demanding because ballet accompanying requires a lot of repetitive motion, especially in the left hand. Unfortunately, this contributed to my chronic tendonitis and ultimately pushed me to reconsider my career path, leading me toward librarianship.

I was a frequent visitor to the Milwaukee Public Library because they had a fabulous sheet music collection that I relied on for ballet class accompaniment. Their downtown central library, with its beautiful multi-story atrium and marble dome, inspired me profoundly. It was actually walking into that space that sparked the idea that maybe I should become a librarian.

I earned my library and information science degree at the University of Wisconsin–Milwaukee, which had a great program and allowed me to stay close to home. While working on my degree, I had a paid internship in their library, which shifted my career trajectory toward academic libraries. Later, I also did adjunct teaching for their Library School, focusing on emerging technologies and web development.

After graduating, I took a one-year appointment as a general reference librarian at the University of Wisconsin–Parkside. There, I was a liaison to humanities and social sciences departments and created the library's first website during the early days of the Web. I also helped put the institution's strong information literacy program largely online, which was a formative experience for me.

The World Wide Web was just beginning to take hold when I was at UW Parkside, and I created the first website for their library. I also helped move their information literacy courses online, which was pioneering work at the time. Later, I taught basic web development and design as an adjunct instructor, learning and adapting as the web evolved in the mid-1990s.

While in Oklahoma, I did some adjunct teaching for the University of Oklahoma, though my main employment was elsewhere. This teaching role complemented my growing experience in academic librarianship and leadership. It was part of my broader professional activities during my time living in Oklahoma.

My principal employment in Oklahoma was at Oklahoma State University, where I eventually became associate dean with oversight of cataloging and technical services. I was involved in significant projects like system migrations from Notice to Voyager and headed a new department called digital library services focused on managing online resources. This was an accidental but rewarding progression into library leadership.

Ed Johnson was the dean of libraries at Oklahoma State who called me into his office to create and lead a new department for managing online resources. He gave me the freedom to define the role and develop programs to help users find digital content. His support was pivotal in my transition into leadership and digital librarianship.

During my time at Oklahoma State, we migrated from the Notice system to Voyager for library management. I was part of the implementation committee because of my involvement with electronic resources. This hands-on experience taught me a great deal about cataloging and systems beyond what I had learned in library school.

Voyager was the library system we migrated to at Oklahoma State University, replacing Notice. I participated in the implementation committee, which expanded my knowledge of cataloging and technical services. This migration was a significant part of my administrative responsibilities in the library.

In 2013, I became dean of libraries at Florida International University in Miami. FIU's strong commitment to transforming students' lives, especially first-generation and first-time college students, deeply resonated with me. The multicultural environment of Miami and the university's mission made this a meaningful and exciting chapter in my career.

Moving to Miami for the dean position at Florida International University was a big change, but my husband's Cuban heritage and our experience with South American cultures helped us embrace the city's multiculturalism. During my interview, I was warned about the challenges of living in Miami, but we made the decision with eyes wide open. The city's diversity and vibrancy have been an important part of my experience at FIU.
